It does so by varying parameters over plausible ranges and computing Morris elementary effects on the scenario deltas relative to baseline [@morris_1991_factorial_sampling; @campolongo_2007_effective]. @fig-05-sensitivity-heatmap aggregates those Morris $\mu^*$ values to four broad mechanism families and normalises each row to sum to one. The figure should therefore be read as showing where sensitivity is concentrated within each intervention–outcome pair, rather than as an absolute comparison across rows.

The concentration pattern differs across intervention types. Institutional-trust repair and legitimacy repair place their largest shares on persistence, consistent with effects that depend strongly on how slowly trust- and legitimacy-related states relax. The norm-shift row tilts most strongly toward social diffusion, in line with the role of reinforcement and network propagation in sustaining prevention, though stochasticity also remains non-negligible. Access facilitation is concentrated most heavily on stochasticity, with additional sensitivity to the incidence loop, probably because it acts more directly on vaccination rather than working through the socially reinforcing parts of the ABM. The NPI-index row shown here is more diffuse, with screening influence spread across persistence, social diffusion, and the incidence loop rather than concentrated in a single family. At the parameter level, the corresponding Morris dispersion statistic $\sigma$ gives very similar rankings overall (median rank correlation $\approx 0.99$), but suggests that persistence and some feedback terms are more context-dependent in the norm-heavy and more socially mediated scenarios.

A complementary claim-level check asks whether selected intervention rankings remain true across the screened parameter space, where $p$ denotes the share of screened parameter draws for which the comparison holds. The clearest pattern is the contrast between cumulative and late-horizon comparisons. Norm shift exceeds institutional-trust repair on both vaccination and NPI cumulative yield in all screened draws ($p=1.00$ for both $ΔAUC$ comparisons), but the corresponding month-12 orderings are essentially unstable ($p=0.44$ for vaccination and $p=0.52$ for NPI, with median gaps near zero). A similar reversal appears against legitimacy repair: norm shift exceeds it on vaccination $ΔAUC$ in $76\%$ of draws, yet legitimacy repair exceeds norm shift on month-12 vaccination in $96\%$ of draws. This is consistent with the ABM’s mean reversion: interventions can separate strongly in cumulative yield while ending at much more similar, or even reversed, late-horizon levels.

Some asymmetries are more robust. Legitimacy repair exceeds institutional-trust repair on vaccination in all screened draws, while institutional-trust repair exceeds legitimacy repair on the NPI outcome in all screened draws. Access facilitation shows a different persistence profile: its vaccination effect always peaks above its month-12 level ($p=1.00$; median final-to-peak ratio $0.61$), but that does not imply a positive cumulative effect, because the later reversal is strong enough that its post-burn vaccination $ΔAUC$ is negative on average in the canonical shock runs. Its month-12 NPI effect is never above baseline. The outbreak comparison is weaker and less consistent. On vaccination, outbreak salience exceeds outbreak wave in most draws ($p=0.78$ for $ΔAUC$ and $p=0.86$ at month 12), while on NPI the wave retains only a marginal edge ($p=0.56$ for both $ΔAUC$ and month 12). This matches the family-level screening: for NPI, outbreak wave is concentrated almost entirely on the incidence loop, whereas outbreak salience is distributed more broadly across stochasticity, incidence feedback, and social diffusion.



# Discussion & Conclusion {#sec-06-discussion-conclusion}

This thesis develops behavioural policy simulation through a three-step pipeline linking empirical evidence, component-level evaluation, and dynamic simulation. It first builds a compact survey-grounded representation of prevention behaviour, then tests whether LLM components can reproduce that structure both at baseline and under controlled shifts, and finally embeds the resulting behavioural core in an ABM. The broader argument is that behavioural policy simulation becomes more credible when the behavioural layer is constrained by more than baseline fit alone. It also needs to respond coherently when the relevant drivers of behaviour are deliberately perturbed.

The results show that prevention is organised by a compact but outcome-specific architecture. COVID vaccination willingness depends mainly on stakes and vaccine evaluation within a wider trust and legitimacy context, flu vaccination is dominated by inertia, and the NPI outcomes are shaped mainly by moral orientation and stakes. Off-the-shelf LLMs can recover part of this structure in static settings, especially in the ranking of baseline profiles, but they respond much less reliably once the profile is shifted. Their weakness is therefore not only imperfect fit, but limited counterfactual discipline. This is why the ABM does not rely on them as the core behavioural update mechanism. Instead, it keeps behaviour tied to the estimated survey relationships and adds a minimal dynamic layer for persistence, social influence, and epidemic feedback. In that simulator, norms generate the most persistent cumulative effects, access improvements can induce compensatory declines in other precautions through reduced incidence pressure, and legitimacy is more vaccination-specific whereas institutional trust spills over more broadly across prevention outcomes.

## Implications {#sec-06-implications}

One implication of this thesis is methodological. In behavioural policy simulators, aggregate epidemic or uptake patterns are not sufficient to validate the behavioural layer, because similar macro-level outcomes can be generated by different underlying micro-level structures. The more demanding test is whether the model responds coherently when the relevant behavioural drivers are deliberately shifted. The LLM results show this concretely: a component can perform plausibly in static reconstruction tasks and still mis-scale intervention responses or diffuse them across dimensions that should remain stable. Baseline fit is therefore an incomplete test of validity.

This matters because the behavioural architecture of prevention is broader than a single risk-response channel. Epidemic policy models often centre changing perceived risk [@funk_2010_modelling; @verelst_2016_behavioural_models], and the results here confirm that appraisal of disease stakes is important, especially for COVID vaccination willingness. But that is only part of the empirical structure. Trust and legitimacy shape whether recommendations are accepted as credible and appropriate. Norms help keep prevention visible in everyday interaction and can sustain behaviour through social reinforcement. Inertia and feasibility constraints help explain why some behaviours remain sticky even when salience changes. A model centred only on perceived danger may therefore capture short-run reactions to worsening conditions, while missing the mechanisms that sustain, block, or redirect prevention over time.

The policy implication is that interventions should be matched to the behavioural margins they can realistically move. Measures aimed at salience or credibility are more likely to work where uptake depends on appraisal and institutional acceptance. By contrast, behaviours anchored more strongly in inertia, feasibility constraints, or stable value orientations are less likely to shift through communication alone. This also means that success on one preventive outcome does not automatically generalise to others. An intervention that raises vaccination may still weaken other precautions if the resulting reduction in perceived risk is not accompanied by forces that sustain broader prevention. In that sense, effective policy design is not only about increasing protection somewhere in the system, but about understanding which behavioural channels are being activated, how persistent they are, and whether gains in one domain may offset behaviour in another.

## Limitations {#sec-06-limitations}

The thesis prioritises interpretability and portability of the behavioural model. Those design choices make the pipeline easier to inspect and evaluate, but they also define its main limitations.

The empirical base has clear limits. The survey is self-reported, cross-national, and fielded after the acute phase of COVID-19. Recall bias, expressive responding, and social desirability may affect several variables, especially perceived norms, moral items, and retrospective vaccination attitudes. Cross-country harmonisation reduces some comparability problems, but measurement invariance is not guaranteed. Some between-country differences may therefore reflect differences in interpretation or response style rather than differences in the underlying psychological profiles.

Observability is also incomplete. The survey captures many important determinants of preventive behaviour, but not all of them. Feasibility constraints that are likely to matter for behaviours such as staying home when symptomatic, including workplace flexibility, paid leave, caregiving obligations, and immediate household logistics, are only partially observed. This likely contributes to the weaker empirical separability of some outcomes and means that the constraint-related variables capture only part of the feasibility conditions that shape these behaviours.

A central limitation is that the empirical backbone is anchored in cross-sectional data. The thesis uses between-person gradients as a local guide to nearby within-person adjustment, while a separate law of motion governs persistence and relaxation. That bridge is useful, but it remains an assumption rather than a direct empirical identification of behavioural dynamics. The data do not reveal individual decay rates, whether every observed profile is dynamically reachable, or the exact persistence of intervention-induced changes. For that reason, the model is more informative about the direction and relative magnitude of responses than about the precise time scale over which they unfold.

There are also limitations in the statistical backbone. The reference equations are deliberately parsimonious and interpretable, which is useful for mechanism-focused simulation, but this comes with functional-form restrictions. Linear and ordinal-link models may smooth over nonlinearities, threshold effects, or subgroup interactions that matter in specific contexts. The resulting perturbation summaries provide principled empirical reference points for evaluation, but they remain model-based estimates, not ground truth. The same applies to block construction: the chosen derived variables are compact summaries of broader constructs, and different measurement choices could slightly alter the resulting empirical structure.

The LLM evaluation has additional scope conditions. Its results are conditional on the tested models, prompt formats, and the controlled perturbation setup used to keep the comparisons interpretable. Other frontier systems or more specialised training procedures may perform differently.

Finally, the ABM is built to compare behavioural policy scenarios, not to forecast epidemic trajectories in a fully epidemiological sense. For that reason, incidence is represented through a simplified local-pressure process. It is not an explicit transmission model. The social layer includes clustered exposure and norm propagation, but the network is fixed and does not allow endogenous rewiring, selective tie dissolution, or the emergence of echo chambers through adaptive sorting. The update mechanism is intentionally modular, which improves interpretability and prevents unconstrained drift, but may understate some real forms of interaction across psychological profile variables.

## Extensions {#sec-06-extensions}

The most useful extensions follow directly from the main limits identified in this thesis. Two priorities stand out: tighter empirical constraints on generative components, and a stronger dynamic backbone for simulation.

On the generative side, the micro-validation harness could be used not only for evaluation but also for optimisation. In this thesis, the perturbation tests are used to assess whether a model reproduces reference responses to controlled shifts, preserves placebo invariances, and avoids unstable spillovers across profile dimensions. Because these targets are automatically checkable, they could also serve as optimisation targets, in line with recent work that trains models against verifiable external criteria [@wen_2025_rlvr_reasoning; @jiang_2026_reward_chain]. A related extension is to separate communication from behavioural updating more explicitly. A more robust design would keep profile evolution in a parametric module, where bounds and stability can be enforced directly, while using an LLM as a social interface for generating messages, interpreting communication, and representing informational or normative exposure in natural language.

On the dynamic side, the current framework could be strengthened in two complementary ways. One is to couple the model to an explicit transmission process. In the present thesis, incidence enters as a stylised local-pressure signal that closes the behavioural feedback loop while keeping the epidemiological side intentionally reduced-form. The contact-network structure developed here would also support a stochastic transmission layer, such as a network-based SIR, SEIR, or SEIRV process [@keeling_2008_modeling_infectious_diseases]. The other is to improve dynamic identification through longitudinal data. Because the present behavioural backbone is anchored in cross-sectional variation, the ABM relies on cross-sectional gradients to connect between-person differences to within-person change. Repeated measurement of the same individuals would strengthen this part of the pipeline substantially by identifying inertia, post-shock decay, habituation, and asymmetric recovery more directly.

Beyond these specific extensions, the same logic could also be made cumulative across studies. A recurring problem in behavioural simulation is that models are often evaluated against bespoke targets and ad hoc criteria, which makes results difficult to compare across settings [@windrum_2007_empirical_validation]. A shared micro-validation framework anchored to survey data offers one way to narrow that gap. Models could then be compared on common tasks that test baseline fit, coherent response to controlled perturbations, and stability under repeated updating, making progress less dependent on isolated demonstrations and more on direct comparison across parametric, generative, and hybrid architectures. The framework developed in this thesis can be read as one step in that direction: it combines a survey-grounded behavioural backbone, component-level micro-validation, and counterfactual simulation within a single pipeline.



# Bibliography {#sec-bibliography .unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::



# Dataset details {#sec-appendix-dataset-details}

The survey was run in 2024 as a self-administered online questionnaire across Italy, Spain, France, Germany, Hungary, and the United Kingdom, using a common instrument translated into the five non-English study languages. Full fieldwork and sampling details are reported in @offeddu_2025_advancing_behavioural_epidemic_data. The published source reports 22,228 respondents after predefined data-quality filters. This thesis instead starts from the harmonised survey extract prior to that final published row-level exclusion and then applies the preprocessing and analysis-sample restrictions described below.

The questionnaire is organised into eight core modules:

1. Demographics, household, and health background: socio-demographics (including education, employment, and income), household composition, and health-risk context for respondent and close others.
2. COVID-19 experience and vaccination history: infection history and COVID-19 vaccination uptake/timing, plus retrospective willingness at key rollout time points and stated motives/barriers.
3. Other infectious diseases and vaccine beliefs: flu/measles/meningitis-related risk and severity perceptions, flu vaccination history, and a short vaccine-knowledge/beliefs module.
4. Information ecosystem: primary media/social channels used, time spent, and trust in vaccine information by channel.
5. Social network, contacts, and discussions: network size by role (e.g., coworkers/friends), a contact matrix for direct contacts yesterday by social group and age band, and a parallel matrix for discussion contacts last week.
6. Attitudes, norms, and trust: political/value items and perceived norms in close groups; institutional trust in vaccine-related information; and the 5C antecedents of vaccination [@betsch_2018_beyond].
7. Accessibility and barriers: self-efficacy and practical constraints for accessing vaccination (distance, availability, time/work conflicts, and related impediments).
8. Moral foundations: adapted Moral Foundations batteries capturing relevance and agreement for the five foundations [@haidt_2004_intuitive; @graham_2011_mapping].


## Data cleaning and dataset preparation {#sec-appendix-dd-data-cleaning}

### Harmonisation and cross-country comparability

Because the questionnaire combines binary items, ordered scales, counts, routed follow-up questions, and some country-specific response formats, the first preprocessing step harmonises these inputs into a common analytic representation. This mainly involved recoding categorical responses into consistent categories, aligning and reverse-coding ordered scales so that higher values had a common interpretation where appropriate, and aggregating conceptually related items into composite summaries, usually by averaging across non-missing responses.

To support cross-country comparison, socio-economic variables were harmonised where possible onto comparable scales. Household income brackets were mapped to a continuous proxy and then converted into a PPP-adjusted numeric measure. Geographic information was likewise harmonised into Eurostat NUTS-style identifiers, with lower-level regional categories rolled up when they were too sparse for stable analysis.

### Missing data and structural non-response

Missing values are retained whenever they plausibly reflect genuine item non-response and may therefore remain analytically informative. The preprocessing workflow nevertheless applies a limited set of structural imputations in cases where survey routing implies a natural default and where leaving the value missing would create internal inconsistencies.

For example, numeric counts derived from household or close-contact rosters are imputed to zero when missingness is consistent with the respondent having reported no such individuals. Similarly, media-use duration items are imputed to “Never” when missingness is consistent with routed non-use, after which they are encoded onto the ordinal duration scale and, where relevant, combined into summary indices. These imputations are intentionally narrow in scope, while other forms of missingness are left untouched.

### Removing unusable variables and treating extremes

Not all raw survey fields are suitable for downstream statistical modelling. Variables are therefore removed when they are redundant, purely administrative, free-text, or too sparse to support stable inference. This includes metadata fields, open-ended “other (specify)” responses, and highly incomplete routed follow-ups.

For high-variance count variables, the preprocessing workflow also applies plausibility bounds and outlier checks so that a very small number of implausible responses do not dominate scale construction or model estimation. This affects variables such as household counts, non-cohabiting family counts, and infection- or contact-related totals, where the survey instrument already uses bounded response ranges and the clean analytic representation preserves those bounds.

## Derived variables, index construction, and measurement quality {#sec-appendix-dd-index-construction}

Many of the variables used in the empirical analyses are not single questionnaire items but derived summaries constructed from conceptually related responses. This section documents how those indices were built and how their measurement quality was assessed.


Where possible, conceptually related items were aggregated by taking the mean across non-missing responses. Formally, for respondent $i$ and an index built from items $j \in J_i$ with observed responses $x_{ij}$, the index is

$$
\text{Index}_i = \frac{1}{\lvert J_i \rvert} \sum_{j \in J_i} x_{ij},
$$

where $J_i$ denotes the set of non-missing items for that respondent. No additional minimum-item requirement is imposed beyond this available-item rule, so some indices may be based on as few as one observed item. I therefore report index coverage and treat lower coverage as an indication that missingness reduces the effective sample size for that composite.

For multi-item composites intended to capture a common latent construct, I report Cronbach’s $\alpha$ as a descriptive reliability diagnostic. For formative composites, such as the 5C pro-vaccine index, $\alpha$ is included for transparency. No internal-consistency statistic is reported for aggregates built from heterogeneous entities, such as household/contact summaries or media-use baskets, because these are deterministic summaries rather than psychometric scales.

### Derived household and social-contact variables

The survey records detailed information on up to ten cohabitants and up to ten non-cohabiting close contacts, including age, COVID-19 vaccination status, and COVID-19 infection history. To avoid a proliferation of highly sparse columns, this information is summarised into a small set of continuous variables:

* Average age of cohabitants/non-cohabitants
* Average number of COVID vaccine doses among cohabitants/non-cohabitants
* Average number of COVID infections among cohabitants/non-cohabitants

### Media use and information diet {#sec-appendix-dd-media-use}

Time spent with different online and social media is summarised into two duration indices:

* Duration of Informational Media averages reported time on informational platforms such as Wikipedia, YouTube, and Reddit.
* Duration of Social Media averages time on social platforms such as Instagram, Facebook, Telegram, Twitter/X, LinkedIn, and TikTok.

### Composite psychological indices {#sec-appendix-dd-composite-indices}

#### 5C pro-vaccine index

To summarise pro-vaccine psychological orientation, I construct the 5C pro-vaccine index (`{python} alpha_md("fivec_pro_vax_idx")`) as the mean of four recoded 5C components:

* 5C Confidence
* 5C Complacency (reverse-coded)
* 5C Constraints (reverse-coded)
* 5C Collective Responsibility

#### Risk, safety, fear and comfort

Perceptions of infection risk and vaccine risk are captured using multi-item averages:

* Average perceived infection likelihood across COVID-19, seasonal flu, measles, and meningitis (`{python} alpha_md("infection_likelihood_avg")`).
* Average perceived severity if infected across COVID-19 and flu (`{python} alpha_md("severity_if_infected_avg")`).
* Average disease fear across COVID-19, seasonal flu, measles, and meningitis (`{python} alpha_md("disease_fear_avg")`).

Although baseline prevalence differs sharply across pathogens, the perceived infection likelihood composite is interpreted as a dispositional tendency to perceive infectious diseases as likely in general, rather than as a calibrated estimate of objective infection probabilities.

The perceived severity index is likewise treated as a general severity sensitivity: how strongly respondents expect serious consequences conditional on infection, rather than a disease-specific clinical assessment.

For vaccines:

* Average perceived vaccine risk across COVID-19, flu, measles, and meningitis vaccines (`{python} alpha_md("vaccine_risk_avg")`).

#### COVID-19 vaccine rollout scepticism

To capture scepticism about the safety, motives, and mandate-related aspects of the COVID-19 vaccination campaign, I construct a COVID-19 vaccine rollout scepticism composite (`{python} alpha_md("covax_legitimacy_scepticism_idx")`) by averaging agreement across six statements covering safety concerns, perceived insufficient testing, side-effect worries, opposition to mandates, distrust in pharmaceutical motives, and preference for natural immunity.

#### Progressive attitudes and social norms

I construct a Progressive attitudes index (`{python} alpha_md("progressive_attitudes_nonvax_idx")`) by averaging agreement with:

* concern about climate harm,
* support for LGBT adoption rights,
* tolerance towards immigration.

Perceived vaccine-importance norms are measured separately as single items capturing how important respondents believe vaccination is to their family, friends, and colleagues.

Three analogous progressive norms bundles capture perceived social climate among friends (`{python} alpha_md("friends_progressive_norms_nonvax_idx")`), family (`{python} alpha_md("family_progressive_norms_nonvax_idx")`), and colleagues (`{python} alpha_md("colleagues_progressive_norms_nonvax_idx")`) by averaging the same items within each reference group.

The progressive attitudes composite has modest internal consistency, as expected given its deliberate coverage of multiple policy domains. It should therefore be interpreted as a broad value gradient rather than a narrow latent trait. Likewise, the progressive norms bundles are treated as descriptive composites rather than narrow psychometric scales, and relatively low Cronbach’s alpha values for these bundles are not taken as evidence of measurement failure but as a reflection of their intentionally broad content.

For vaccination specifically, I also combine the three vaccine-importance norm items into a single Perceived vaccine norms index, defined as their mean (`{python} alpha_md("social_norms_vax_avg")`). I do so because these three items display very high internal consistency, indicating that perceived vaccine importance is largely shared across respondents’ close reference groups rather than being sharply group-specific.

Finally, to summarise a broader moral–progressive orientation that links progressive policy attitudes to individualising moral concerns (care and fairness), I construct a higher-order composite by averaging the Progressive attitudes index with the two individualising morality constructs (moral relevance and moral agreement). The resulting composite is reported with its internal-consistency diagnostic (`{python} alpha_md("moral_progressive_orientation")`).

#### Protective behaviour bundle

The protective behaviour index (`{python} alpha_md("protective_behaviour_avg")`) summarises three non-pharmaceutical behavioural items:

* wearing a mask in crowded places when symptomatic,
* staying at home when symptomatic,
* wearing a mask when “pressure” (infection risk) is high.

### Trust indices {#sec-appendix-dd-trust-indices}

Trust in information sources is summarised at three levels:

* Trust in traditional media (`{python} alpha_md("trust_traditional")`)
  Mean of trust in printed press, television, and radio as sources of vaccine information.
* Trust in online information (`{python} alpha_md("trust_onlineinfo")`)
  Average trust in online news websites, Wikipedia, YouTube, and podcasts as vaccine information sources.
* Trust in social media (`{python} alpha_md("trust_social")`)
  Average trust in Facebook, Instagram, Twitter/X, TikTok, Telegram, and LinkedIn.

Finally, the Institutional trust index (`{python} alpha_md("institutional_trust_avg")`) averages trust in local and national governments, general practitioners, local and national health authorities, and the WHO.

### Moral foundations indices {#sec-appendix-dd-moral-foundations}

Moral values were measured using an adapted version of the Moral Foundations Questionnaire (MFQ), which distinguishes between five moral foundations: care, fairness, loyalty, authority, and purity [@haidt_2004_intuitive; @graham_2011_mapping]. Following standard practice, I construct separate indices for relevance and agreement items within each foundation, and further aggregate them into higher-order individualizing (care, fairness) and binding (loyalty, authority, purity) dimensions. These higher-order composites are used in the main analyses, while foundation-specific subscales are treated as exploratory.

For each foundation, I construct:

* Relevance subscales, each averaging two items about how morally relevant a given consideration is:

  * Care relevance (`{python} alpha_md("mfq_relevance_care_idx")`),
  * Fairness relevance (`{python} alpha_md("mfq_relevance_fairness_idx")`),
  * Loyalty relevance (`{python} alpha_md("mfq_relevance_loyalty_idx")`),
  * Authority relevance (`{python} alpha_md("mfq_relevance_authority_idx")`),
  * Purity relevance (`{python} alpha_md("mfq_relevance_purity_idx")`)

* Agreement subscales, each averaging two items about agreement with value statements:

  * Care agreement (`{python} alpha_md("mfq_agreement_care_idx")`),
  * Fairness agreement (`{python} alpha_md("mfq_agreement_fairness_idx")`),
  * Loyalty agreement (`{python} alpha_md("mfq_agreement_loyalty_idx")`),
  * Authority agreement (`{python} alpha_md("mfq_agreement_authority_idx")`),
  * Purity agreement (`{python} alpha_md("mfq_agreement_purity_idx")`)

To align with standard MFQ practice, I then form higher-order individualizing and binding indices:

* Relevance–individualizing index (`{python} alpha_md("mfq_relevance_individualizing_idx")`) averages care and fairness relevance,
* Relevance–binding index (`{python} alpha_md("mfq_relevance_binding_idx")`) averages loyalty, authority, and purity relevance,
* Agreement–individualizing index (`{python} alpha_md("mfq_agreement_individualizing_idx")`) averages care and fairness agreement,
* Agreement–binding index (`{python} alpha_md("mfq_agreement_binding_idx")`) averages loyalty, authority, and purity agreement.

As is typical for two-item subscales, some foundation-specific reliability estimates are modest. The main analyses therefore rely primarily on the higher-order individualizing and binding composites.



# Missing-data handling and comparability of fit statistics {#sec-appendix-missing-data}

Because some predictors are missing by design, especially in conditionally administered modules, naive listwise deletion would cause different model specifications and LOBO comparisons to be estimated on different respondent sets. This is problematic because likelihood-based fit statistics, including BIC, are only directly comparable when they are computed on the same rows. To preserve comparability across specifications, the modelling workflow applies a common missing-data policy to predictors and reports the resulting sample sizes.


For any predictor with missingness fraction at or below a threshold $\tau$ (here `{python} tau_md()`), I retain the variable, add a missingness indicator $M=\mathbb{1}(X\ \text{missing})$, and fill missing values using a type-aware rule. Binary items are filled with 0 and flagged; roughly symmetric scales or indices are mean-filled, which is equivalent to 0 after z-scoring; and highly skewed continuous measures, such as income, are median-filled, typically within country. Predictors with missingness above $\tau$ are excluded from the relevant specification. Rows are dropped only for missing outcomes or non-finite values. This approach keeps estimation samples aligned across comparable models while treating non-response as part of the observed data structure rather than eliminating it through silent row deletion.



# Additional modelling results {#sec-appendix-modeling}


::: {.content-visible when-format="pdf"}
```{=latex}
\begin{landscape}
```
:::



::: {.content-visible when-format="pdf"}
```{=latex}
\end{landscape}
```
:::



# Derivation of PE for GLM {#sec-appendix-ice-glm-derivation}

I derive the two-point ICE contrast, and hence the corresponding reference perturbation effect (PE), under a GLM decision rule, making explicit its dependence on the estimated slope $\beta_X$, the lever step $\Delta x$, and, through the inverse link, the baseline index level. Let the fitted rule be written as
$$
f(x,z)=g^{-1}\big(\eta(x,z)\big),
\qquad
\eta(x,z)=\alpha+\beta_X x+\gamma^\top z,
$$
where $x$ is the lever value and $z$ collects all remaining covariates held fixed in the ICE thought experiment. For a shift $x_0\to x_1$ with $\Delta x=x_1-x_0$ and baseline index $\eta_0=\eta(x_0,z)$, the latent-scale displacement is
$$
\Delta\eta=\eta(x_1,z)-\eta(x_0,z)=\beta_X\Delta x,
$$
which is exact and independent of $z$ (the intercept and $\gamma^\top z$ cancel in the difference). The associated two-point ICE contrast is therefore
$$
f(x_1,z)-f(x_0,z)=g^{-1}(\eta_0+\Delta\eta)-g^{-1}(\eta_0)= g^{-1}(\eta_0+\beta_X\Delta x)-g^{-1}(\eta_0).
$$

In the proportional-odds cumulative logit reference models used in the thesis,
$$
\operatorname{logit}\Pr(Y\le k\mid x,z)=\tau_k-\eta(x,z),\qquad k=1,\dots,K-1,
$$
so a lever shift $x_0\to x_1$ implies the same additive change for every cumulative logit:
$$
\operatorname{logit}\Pr(Y\le k\mid x_1,z)-\operatorname{logit}\Pr(Y\le k\mid x_0,z)=(\eta(x_1,z)-\eta(x_0,z))=\beta_X\Delta x.
$$
Outcome-scale contrasts for any category probability or summary derived from these probabilities are then induced by this common latent displacement and depend on the baseline index $\eta_0$ only through the link function.



# Screening off-manifold perturbations {#sec-appendix-offmanifold-conditional-percentile}

The country-specific conditional-tail diagnostic below screens perturbations for empirical plausibility. Because psychological profile variables are correlated in the observed profile space, setting a single lever $X$ to $x_{\text{low}}$ or $x_{\text{high}}$ while holding the remaining covariates $z_i$ fixed can generate combinations that are rarely observed in the survey. I flag such perturbed points using a conditional tail probability based on $\hat F(X \mid z)$.

For each country $c$ and lever $X$, I estimate the conditional distribution of $X$ given the remaining profile:
$$
\hat F_c(x\mid z)=\Pr\bigl(X\le x \mid Z=z,\ \text{country}=c\bigr).
$$

Given an intervention value $x^*$ applied to respondent $i$ in country $c(i)$, I compute its conditional percentile $\pi_i(x^*)$. For continuous $X$, this is
$$
\pi_i(x^*)=\hat F_{c(i)}(x^*\mid z_i).
$$

For discrete $X$ (ordinal or binary), I use a mid-CDF percentile to avoid degeneracy at the endpoints:
$$
\pi_i(x^*)=\Pr(X<x^*\mid z_i,c(i))+\tfrac{1}{2}\Pr(X=x^*\mid z_i,c(i)).
$$

I then convert $\pi_i(x^*)$ into a two-sided conditional tail probability,
$$
\mathrm{tail}_i(x^*) = 2\min\{\pi_i(x^*),\,1-\pi_i(x^*)\},
$$
and I flag the perturbed point $(x^*,z_i)$ as off-manifold if
$$
\mathrm{tail}_i(x^*) < 0.05.
$$

Conditional on $z_i$ within country $c(i)$, the assigned value $x^*$ lies in the most extreme $2.5\%$ of the conditional distribution on at least one side.

All conditional models are fit within country. For continuous levers, I estimate a grid of conditional quantiles and use monotone interpolation to approximate $\hat F_c(x \mid z)$. For ordinal levers, I use an ordered response model to recover the conditional probabilities entering the mid-CDF percentile. For binary levers, I use a logistic model and derive the same percentile measure from the predicted category probabilities.

When I evaluate perturbation effects for lever $X$ on respondent $i$, I create two perturbed points, $(x_{\text{low}},z_i)$ and $(x_{\text{high}},z_i)$. I compute $\mathrm{tail}_i(x_{\text{low}})$ and $\mathrm{tail}_i(x_{\text{high}})$ and flag the perturbation as off-manifold if either endpoint is extreme:
$$
\mathrm{off\_manifold}_{i,X} = \mathbb{1}\left(
\min\{\mathrm{tail}_i(x_{\text{low}}),\,\mathrm{tail}_i(x_{\text{high}})\} < 0.05
\right).
$$

The screen is therefore conservative: a perturbation is retained only if both the low and high endpoint remain conditionally plausible given the rest of the respondent’s profile.



# Anchor prototypes for LLM evaluation and ABM resampling {#sec-appendix-anchor-prototypes}

Anchor prototypes provide a compact empirical scaffold for both the LLM evaluation and the ABM initialisation. Rather than working directly with the full respondent cloud, I select a small set of observed profile prototypes that cover the main gradients of the psychological-profile space.

Let $z_i\in\mathbb{R}^p$ denote respondent $i$'s vector of core psychological profile variables. To remove country-level baseline differences while preserving within-country structure, each variable is standardised after country demeaning:
$$
z'_{ij} = \frac{z_{ij}-\bar z_{c(i)j}}{s_j},
$$
where $\bar z_{c(i)j}$ is the mean of variable $j$ in respondent $i$'s country and $s_j$ is a pooled scale.

Anchors are selected in three steps. First, the standardised profile space is projected to a lower-dimensional representation using principal components analysis, so that distance calculations are driven by the main empirical gradients rather than by noise in a high-dimensional space [@jolliffe_2002_principal]. Second, a small set of observed respondent profiles is chosen as anchor prototypes using k-medoids in that reduced space [@kaufman_2005_finding]. Third, the final anchor set is required to have support across multiple countries, so that anchors represent recurrent cross-country profile types rather than country-specific outliers.

## Coverage diagnostics {#sec-appendix-anchoring-results}




The anchor construction is intended to provide broad empirical coverage, and I report two diagnostics to assess whether it does so. First, @tbl-coverage reports nearest-anchor distances by country in the normalised anchoring space. These distances are similar across countries, indicating that no country is systematically under-covered under the chosen anchor specification. Second, anchors should not collapse into country clusters; they should instead capture profile types that recur across countries. The cross-country support diagnostic points in that direction: anchors typically receive non-trivial support from multiple countries. On average, about `{python} fmt_math(fmt_float(mean_countries_above, 1))` of the 6 countries contribute above the support threshold for a given anchor.



# Example prompt templates for LLM micro-validation {#sec-appendix-llm-prompts}

## Profile presentation formats {#sec-appendix-prompt-families}

The first source of variation concerns how the respondent profile is presented to the model. In the standard prompting families, the underlying information is held fixed and only the surface rendering changes. The examples below are only representative: the full profile shown to the model varies across tasks and outcomes, so additional fields may be present or some fields may differ.

The data-only format presents the respondent as a structured list of attributes with explicit scales and values. This is the closest format to the underlying tabular survey record.

```text
Survey context: Country=Germany, Year=2024

Attributes:

Scale: 1–5 (1 = Not at all, 5 = Extremely)
- Trust in vaccine information (by source):
  - Institutions/public authorities (local/national gov, GP, health authorities, national health institutions, WHO): 3
- Flu vaccine perceived risk: 1
- Flu disease fear: 1

Scale: 1–7 (1 = Strongly disagree, 7 = Strongly agree)
- 5C pro-vaccine orientation index: 5.5
- Perceived social norms: "vaccines are important": 5

- Age: 68
- Income (PPP-normalised) (scale 445.994–4795.962): 720.835
- Education level (0 = ED0–2; 1 = ED3–4; 2 = ED5–8): 0
- Any cohabitants have medical conditions: Yes
- Experienced vaccine side effects (self): No
```

The third-person format renders the same profile as a short vignette. It preserves the same respondent information, but translates it into a compact descriptive narrative.

```text
It is 2024.
They are a woman, in their 20s, with a higher level of formal education.
Their health is not great; they have had COVID twice; vaccine side effects have affected them and people they know.
Overall, they feel neutral about vaccine information; they trust traditional media much more than the national government.
Vaccination comes up often in conversations at home.
Overall, they feel mixed about vaccines; they are not very confident vaccines are safe; protecting others matters a lot to them here.
People around them lean against vaccines.
They endorse principles of loyalty, authority, and purity, and these matter somewhat when they judge right and wrong.
```

The first-person format renders the respondent profile as a self-description in the first person and injects it as an assistant message. The subsequent user prompt then asks for a prediction, while the system instruction requires the model to treat the injected self-description as its own prior statement and remain consistent with it.

```text
You are a human survey respondent answering in the first person.
You are not an AI system, nor a chatbot.

Do not use meta-language about being an AI, a model, or a system.

You are answering as the same speaker across the conversation.
Maintain strict self-consistency with what you have already stated about yourself, your beliefs, and your constraints in prior assistant messages.
Do not add new biography, motives, or beliefs unless they have been stated already. If something is not stated, treat it as unknown.

Treat prior narrative statements as your own prior statements.
```

A representative injected assistant message is:

```text
It is 2024.
I am a woman, in my 20s, with a higher level of formal education.
My health is not great; I have had COVID twice; vaccine side effects have affected me and people I know.
Overall, I feel mixed about vaccines; I am not very confident vaccines are safe.
These statements describe me; I stand by them for the next question.
```

The paired-profile format is used only in perturbed tasks. Instead of showing a single profile, it presents two versions of the same respondent, one at the low value of the focal lever and one at the high value, while all other attributes are held fixed.

A representative paired-profile prompt is:

```text
Survey context: Country=Germany, Year=2024

Common attributes:
- Flu vaccine perceived risk: 1
- Flu disease fear: 1

Scale: 1–7 (1 = Strongly disagree, 7 = Strongly agree)
- 5C pro-vaccine orientation index: 5.5
- Perceived social norms: "vaccines are important": 5

- Age: 68
- Income (PPP-normalised) (scale 445.994–4795.962): 720.835
- Education level (0 = ED0–2; 1 = ED3–4; 2 = ED5–8): 0
- Any cohabitants have medical conditions: Yes
- Experienced vaccine side effects (self): No

Profile A (differences):
- Institutional trust in vaccine information: 3.5

Profile B (differences):
- Institutional trust in vaccine information: 2.0

Predict each outcome for Profile A and for Profile B separately.
Output format: {"A": {...}, "B": {...}}
```

This format makes the counterfactual comparison explicit inside the prompt itself.

## Prediction targets and output schemas {#sec-appendix-prompt-targets}

The second source of variation concerns the prediction target. The same profile rendering can be paired with different output instructions depending on whether the model is asked to predict a behavioural outcome or a psychological profile variable. In both cases, the natural-language instruction was paired with a structured output constraint: the model was required to return only JSON, and the call also supplied a corresponding JSON Schema specifying the allowed keys and value types.

In the behavioural-outcome tasks, the model predicts one or more preventive outcomes from a fixed survey-grounded respondent profile.

A representative system instruction for a single-outcome behavioural-outcome task is:

```text
You are an expert survey psychometrician acting as a prediction function.

Predict the truthful expected self-reported survey response for the person with the following attributes, not what is socially desirable or recommended.
Fields not listed are unknown; treat them as unknown (do not assume typical values).

Outcome semantics: Flu vaccinated (2023/2024) (0–1: 0 = No, 1 = Yes)

Do not include reasoning or commentary.
Return ONLY JSON: {"p_yes": <float in [0,1]>}.
```

In the psychological-profile tasks, the model predicts the respondent’s psychological profile variables from upstream signals and background traits.

A representative system instruction is:

```text
You are an expert survey psychometrician acting as a prediction function.

Predict the expected self-reported values of the psychological profile variables from the provided predictors.
Fields not listed are unknown; treat them as unknown (do not assume typical values).

Return ONLY valid JSON with keys from the psychological profile.
Use floats for all values.
Include ALL psychological profile keys in every response.
Do not include any other keys, reasoning, or commentary.

Variable semantics:
- covid_perceived_danger_t12: COVID perceived disease danger when offered the first and second dose (scale: 1–5; 1 = Not at all, 5 = Extremely)
- institutional_trust_avg: Trust in institutions/public authorities (local/national gov, GP, health authorities, national health institutions, WHO) (scale: 1–5; 1 = Not at all, 5 = Extremely)
- ...
```

In the paired-profile versions, the prompt additionally makes clear that differences in predictors do not mechanically require differences in the predicted target:

```text
Differences in the predictors do not necessarily imply differences in the predicted psychological profile variables.
```

## Multi-expert decomposition {#sec-appendix-prompt-multi-expert}

The multi-expert strategy differs from the other profile-presentation formats because it changes the elicitation procedure itself, not only the surface rendering of the respondent profile. Instead of asking for one direct prediction from a single prompt, the model is asked to produce several scoped partial judgements from different expert perspectives. Each expert is restricted to a specific mechanism and returns only the contribution attributable to that scope. The final prediction is then constructed from these scoped outputs.

In the behavioural-outcome tasks, the same respondent profile was shown to three experts, each assigned to a different mechanism block. All experts followed the same prompt template. They were asked to use only the evidence within their assigned scope, to ignore all other profile information, and to return only the signed contribution of their block to the final outcome, measured in outcome-scale units relative to a neutral reference profile.

The shared template was:

```text
You are a [EXPERT ROLE].

Task:
- Output ONLY the signed delta contribution of the [MECHANISM] channel to the given survey outcome, in outcome-scale units.

Definition of delta_raw:
- Consider a neutral reference profile where ONLY your scope predictors are at their scale midpoint (or 0 if z-scored). delta_raw is the expected change in the outcome when moving from that neutral profile to the observed profile, using ONLY your scope.

Allowed evidence (scope):
- [ALLOWED EVIDENCE]

Forbidden (do NOT use):
- [FORBIDDEN EVIDENCE]
````

The required output format was:

```json
{"delta_raw": <float>, "mechanism": "<=(8 words)", "fields_used": ["..."]}
```

If the relevant scope fields were absent or unknown, the expert returned `delta_raw = 0.0`.

The three behavioural-outcome experts differed only in role and evidentiary scope. The first expert was framed as a *Behavioural Epidemiologist and Public Health scientist* and was responsible for the disease stakes / exposure channel.

The second expert was framed as a *Social Psychologist specializing in social norms, moralized obligation, and legitimacy / authority acceptance* and was responsible for the norms / moral obligation / legitimacy channel.

The third expert was framed as a *Behavioural Economist focused on practical frictions, constraints, and follow-through* and was responsible for the practical constraints / frictions channel.

For the psychological-profile tasks, the decomposition followed the same general idea, but the target changed. Here the experts no longer contributed partial shifts to a behavioural outcome. Instead, each expert was assigned a subset of the psychological profile and asked to return only the signed post-minus-pre update induced by the signal for the variables in that subset.

All experts again followed a shared template:

```text
You are a [EXPERT ROLE].

Task:
- Output ONLY the signed delta updates (post - pre) to the psychological profile variables in your scope caused by the signal.

Your scope:
[ALLOWED VARIABLES]

Allowed evidence:
[ALLOWED EVIDENCE]

Forbidden:
[FORBIDDEN EVIDENCE]
```

The required output format was:

```json
{"delta": {...}, "fields_used": ["..."]}
```

If the expert judged that the signal produced no meaningful change within its scope, it returned an empty delta object.

The three psychological-profile experts again differed only in role, assigned dimensions, and evidentiary scope. The first expert was framed as a *Public Health and Risk Communication expert* and was responsible for the parts of the profile concerning institutional trust, vaccine-risk perceptions, and legitimacy-related scepticism.

The second expert was framed as a *Social Psychologist specializing in social norms and interpersonal influence* and was responsible for the parts of the profile concerning perceived social norms and trust in vaccine-related information from close social contacts.

The third expert was framed as a *Behavioural Epidemiologist and Behavioural Economist focused on disease stakes and practical frictions* and was responsible for the parts of the profile concerning perceived disease danger and low constraints on action.



# Additional unperturbed LLM micro-validation results {#sec-appendix-llm-micro-validation}

## Choice reconstruction diagnostics {#sec-appendix-choice-validation}





## Psychological-profile reconstruction by variable {#sec-appendix-state-validation}






# ABM specification {#sec-appendix-abm-specification}

The equations below give the mathematical specification of the agent-based model used in @sec-05-agent-based-model.

Throughout, agents are indexed by $i \in \{1,\dots,N\}$ and monthly ticks by $t \in \{0,\dots,T-1\}$. Each agent carries fixed attributes $a_i$, mutable behavioural states $s_i(t)$, and behavioural outcomes $y_i(t)$. The $u$-th state component is written $s_{i,u}(t)$, and the $g$-th outcome component is written $y_{i,g}(t)$. When a construct-specific index is needed in the equations below, I use $c$ for the corresponding state component. Time-varying exogenous context or scenario inputs are denoted $r_i(t)$.

## Agent representation and empirical initialization

The ABM is anchored to the survey, but agents are not initialised by uniform sampling from the raw respondent pool. Instead, each simulated agent is drawn from a local anchor-country neighbourhood. This preserves empirically coherent profiles while making the initial population less dependent on the idiosyncrasies of a single raw subsample and consistent with the anchor-based representation used in the evaluation pipeline.

Each agent first draws an anchor prototype conditional on country:
$$
A_i \sim \operatorname{Categorical}(\pi_{\cdot \mid c_i}).
$$

A donor respondent is then drawn from the neighbourhood $\mathcal N(A_i,c_i)$ using inverse-rank weights:
$$
w(r)\propto \frac{1}{\max\{1,\operatorname{rank}(r)\}},
\qquad
R_i\sim \operatorname{Categorical}(w(\cdot)).
$$

The donor row is copied into the simulated population:
$$
(a_i,s_i(0),y_i(0))\leftarrow (a_{R_i},s_{R_i},y_{R_i}).
$$

This resampling step keeps the initial population on-manifold by construction.

Beyond their fixed attributes $a_i$, initial behavioural states $s_i(0)$, and initial behavioural outcomes $y_i(0)$, agents also receive a small set of fixed response parameters. These capture persistent differences in how strongly agents react inside the social-influence layer:

- $\lambda_i$ (social susceptibility): how strongly the agent updates descriptive vaccination norms in response to observed behaviour.
- $\alpha_i$ (social attention): how likely the agent is to notice and register prevention behaviour in realised contacts.
- $\phi_i$ (exposure intensity): how much the agent is exposed to opportunities for observing others’ behaviour.
- $\theta_i$ (norm threshold): the exposure level at which descriptive norms begin to move substantially; lower values imply easier activation, higher values imply greater resistance.

The first three parameters are survey-anchored. The last, $\theta_i$, is prior-drawn.

The survey anchors are built from three empirical drivers. Two are low-dimensional axes extracted from the survey, and the third is a raw conversation-count measure.

The first axis, social activity, summarizes how socially connected and conversation-active the respondent is. It is the first principal component of:

- number of vaccine conversations with friends,
- number of vaccine conversations with cohabitants,
- number of vaccine conversations with others,
- friend-group size,
- colleague-group size.

The second axis, media exposure, summarizes media use and trust in online or social information channels. It is the first principal component of:

- time spent on social media,
- time spent on information media,
- trust in social media,
- trust in online information.

Using these survey-derived quantities, the runtime defines three drivers:
$$
d_i^{(\lambda)} = \text{social activity}_i + \text{media exposure}_i,
$$
$$
d_i^{(\alpha)} = \text{media exposure}_i + 0.5\,\text{social activity}_i,
$$
$$
d_i^{(\phi)} = \text{conversation count}_i,
$$
where conversation count is the raw total
$$
\text{conversation count}_i
\begin{aligned}
&= \text{conversations with friends}_i \\
&\quad + \text{conversations with cohabitants}_i \\
&\quad + \text{conversations with others}_i.
\end{aligned}
$$

The $\lambda_i$ driver combines social activity and media exposure because norm susceptibility should be higher for agents who are both more socially embedded and more exposed to circulating behavioural signals. The $\alpha_i$ driver puts greater weight on media exposure because attention to others’ behaviour depends more directly on information intake, while still allowing socially active agents to register more cues. The $\phi_i$ driver is tied to raw conversation count because exposure opportunities are most directly proxied by the volume of vaccine-related interpersonal exchange.

Each driver is then mapped into a percentile using an empirical CDF fitted on the survey:
$$
u_i^{(\lambda)}=\widehat F_{\lambda}\!\left(d_i^{(\lambda)}\right),\qquad
u_i^{(\alpha)}=\widehat F_{\alpha}\!\left(d_i^{(\alpha)}\right),\qquad
u_i^{(\phi)}=\widehat F_{\phi}\!\left(d_i^{(\phi)}\right).
$$

These percentiles are then transformed into the runtime coefficients $\lambda_i$, $\alpha_i$, and $\phi_i$.

The remaining parameter, $\theta_i$, which determines how much observed adoption is needed before norms start to shift, is drawn from a symmetric Beta prior:
$$
u_i^{(\theta)}\sim \operatorname{Beta}(a_\theta,a_\theta),
$$
and then mapped into the admissible range,
$$
\theta_i=\theta_{\min}+(\theta_{\max}-\theta_{\min})u_i^{(\theta)}.
$$

## Time-varying state targets and partial-adjustment dynamics

The dynamic part of the ABM is carried by the mutable behavioural states. Rather than changing discontinuously between periods, each construct adjusts only partially toward a time-varying target.

For constructs equipped with a state reference model, the simulator first computes a reference level from the agent’s fixed attributes, current exogenous context inputs, and current state values:
$$
s^\star_{ic}(t)=f_c\big(a_i,r_i(t),s_i(t)\big).
$$

Because the initialized donor state value may lie above or below this fitted reference, the simulator stores the baseline deviation
$$
u_{ic}=s_{ic}(0)-s^\star_{ic}(0),
$$
and adds it back to the moving reference:
$$
\tilde s^\star_{ic}(t)=s^\star_{ic}(t)+u_{ic}.
$$

The target for construct $c$ is then defined as
$$
T_{ic}(t)=
\begin{cases}
\tilde s^\star_{ic}(t), & \text{if a state reference model is available},\\[4pt]
s_{ic}(0), & \text{otherwise},
\end{cases}
$$
with any active incidence-feedback shifts, scenario offsets, or direct state-target overrides applied on top of this baseline target.

State evolution then follows a partial-adjustment law:
$$
\hat s_{ic}(t+1)
=
s_{ic}(t)+\rho_c\big(T_{ic}(t)-s_{ic}(t)\big),
$$
where $\rho_c$ is a construct-specific adjustment rate, taking a fast or slow value depending on the class of behavioural state.

Finally, the updated value is clipped to the admissible range:
$$
s_{ic}(t+1)=\operatorname{clip}_c\big(\hat s_{ic}(t+1)\big).
$$

After the primitive behavioural states are updated, any derived composites are recomputed deterministically. For example, a pro-vaccination index is rebuilt as the mean of its component constructs.

## Social interaction

The ABM also includes social interaction, operating through recurring and one-shot contacts. Social influence enters through two separate channels. One channel pulls connected agents toward one another on selected states. The other updates descriptive norms from observed preventive behaviour. Because social interaction is assumed to evolve faster than the monthly state update, these social processes operate weekly, so each monthly tick contains four social-interaction rounds.

### Recurring contacts

Each agent inherits a locality identifier from the respondent’s NUTS-level location recorded in the survey. Conditional on these locality assignments, the simulator builds a static recurring-contact scaffold with two components.

The first component is a layer of non-household recurring contacts. Its target degree is initialized from a survey-anchored group-size score. Let $g_i$ denote the respondent’s normalized group-size score, derived from friend- and colleague-group sizes. The corresponding non-household core degree is
$$
d_i
=
\operatorname{clip}_{[4,30]}\!\left(
\operatorname{round}\!\left(4+26\,g_i\right)
\right).
$$

The second component is a layer of household ties, built separately from the household-size information. The full recurring-contact set is the union of the two layers:
$$
\mathcal N_i^{\text{rec}}=(\mathcal N_i^{\text{core}}\cup \mathcal N_i^{\text{hh}}).
$$

Neighbour assignments are then sampled without replacement under the locality-mixing rule shared by the static network construction: with probability $1-b$, a candidate is drawn from the same locality, and with probability $b$, from the full population.

### Pairwise peer pull

Peer pull operates on stable social ties in the recurring-contact layer. It applies only to a restricted subset of behavioural states: trust and credibility channels plus the vaccine-risk appraisal channel. Descriptive norms are excluded from peer pull, because they are updated separately from observed behaviour, and broader disease-stakes constructs are also excluded, since they move through the incidence-feedback mechanism.

The probability of interaction with recurring contacts is weighted by homophily. The homophily score is based on a distance metric that combines differences in trust-related and moral-value-related positioning:
$$
D_{ij}=w_T|T_j-T_i|+w_M|M_j-M_i|,
\qquad
W_{ij}=e^{-\gamma_h D_{ij}}.
$$

Here, $T_i$ denotes the agent’s trust-related position and $M_i$ the agent’s moral-value position. Smaller distances imply more similar agents and therefore higher interaction weight.

For each peer-pulled construct $c$, the interaction updates both agents symmetrically:
$$
s_{ic}' = s_{ic}+g_c(s_{jc}-s_{ic}),
\qquad
s_{jc}' = s_{jc}+g_c(s_{ic}-s_{jc}).
$$

### Observed adoption and descriptive-norm updating

Descriptive norms are updated from observed behaviour in the agent’s local social environment. The model distinguishes two separate norm channels: one for vaccination and one for non-pharmaceutical prevention (NPI).

For the vaccination channel, the observed-adoption index is simply the normalized vaccination-willingness outcome:
$$
p_i^{\text{vax}}(t)=\tilde y_{i,\text{vax}}(t).
$$

For the NPI channel, the observed-adoption index is the mean of the three normalized NPI outcomes:
$$
p_i^{\text{npi}}(t)
=
\frac{1}{3}\Big(
\tilde y_{i,\text{mask symptomatic}}(t)
+
\tilde y_{i,\text{stay home symptomatic}}(t)
+
\tilde y_{i,\text{mask high pressure}}(t)
\Big).
$$

Let $\hat p_i^{\text{vax}}(t)$ and $\hat p_i^{\text{npi}}(t)$ denote the resulting exposure estimates for the vaccination and NPI channels, respectively. Each is formed from observed behaviour in the agent’s recurring and one-shot contacts, and each is normalized to $(0,1)$.

Observed exposure is converted into a target descriptive norm in two steps. First, the model asks whether the observed level of adoption is high enough to count as socially meaningful for agent $i$. This is governed by the agent-specific threshold $\theta_i$: lower values mean that even modest visible uptake can shift norms, while higher values require stronger visible uptake. The parameter $\kappa$ controls how sharply the response turns on around that threshold.

For each channel, the normalized exposure estimate $\hat p_i(t)$ is therefore passed through a threshold-style logistic transform:
$$
q_i(t)
=
\sigma\!\left(
\kappa\big(\operatorname{logit}(\hat p_i(t))-\operatorname{logit}(\theta_i)\big)
\right).
$$

The resulting quantity $q_i(t)\in(0,1)$ can be read as the level of socially perceived adoption after thresholding. It is then mapped back into the scale of the descriptive-norm construct:
$$
T_i(t)=\underline s+(\bar s-\underline s)\,q_i(t),
$$

As with the other constructs above, the descriptive norm does not jump directly to this target; it moves only part of the way toward it at each update:

$$
s_i(t+1)
=
\operatorname{clip}\!\left(
s_i(t)
+
\lambda_i^{\text{eff}}\big(T_i(t)-s_i(t)\big)
+
\varepsilon_i(t)
\right),
$$
where
$$
\lambda_i^{\text{eff}}=\operatorname{clip}(\lambda_i\,k_{\text{social}},0,1),
\qquad
\varepsilon_i(t)\sim\mathcal N(0,\sigma_{\text{social}}^2).
$$

Here $\lambda_i^{\text{eff}}$ is the effective adjustment rate, obtained by scaling the agent’s baseline social susceptibility $\lambda_i$ introduced above. Larger values make the agent more responsive to visible behaviour in the social environment, while smaller values make norm updating more inertial. The noise term $\varepsilon_i(t)$ allows small idiosyncratic variation around this deterministic pull toward the target.

## Endogenous incidence feedback

The ABM closes the behavioural loop through a group-level incidence signal defined on the same territorial grouping used in the network layer. Let $g(i)$ denote the locality group of agent $i$, inherited from the respondent’s NUTS-level location in the survey.

Incidence evolves at the level of these groups according to a bounded growth-with-decay process:
$$
I_{g}(t+1)
=
\operatorname{clip}_{[0,1]}\!\Big(
(1-\gamma)I_{g}(t)+\beta I_{g}(t)(1-I_{g}(t))M_{g}(t)+F_{g}(t)
\Big),
$$

The mitigation factor depends on average protective propensity within group $g$:
$$
M_g(t)
=
\operatorname{clip}_{[0,1]}\!\left(
1-\alpha_v\bar v_g(t)-\alpha_p\bar p_g(t)
\right).
$$

The first term carries forward part of the previous incidence level, with $\gamma$ governing how quickly incidence decays. The second term is a logistic-style growth component, where $\beta$ sets the baseline growth rate and $M_g(t)$ scales that growth down when local protective propensity is higher.

Here $\bar v_{g}(t)$ is mean normalized vaccination willingness in group $g$, $\bar p_{g}(t)$ is mean normalized NPI uptake in group $g$, and $F_{g}(t)$ is the exogenous forcing specified by the current scenario, including importation floors, pulses, or local bumps.

Because the survey target is vaccination willingness rather than realised uptake, $\bar v_g(t)$ should be interpreted as an uptake-propensity proxy. Its use in the incidence equation is therefore a modelling choice that prioritises behavioural anchoring over epidemiological realism.

Local incidence feeds back into the behavioural state through a stakes offset:
$$
\Delta_i^{\text{stakes}}(t)=k_{\text{fb}}\big(I_{g(i)}(t)-I_0\big),
$$
where $I_{g(i)}(t)$ is the incidence level in agent $i$'s group, $I_0$ is the baseline incidence anchor, and $k_{\text{fb}}$ controls how strongly deviations from that baseline are translated into behavioural salience.

## Monthly runtime schedule

Each monthly tick follows the same sequence.

1. Activate the current scenario inputs.
   The simulator applies the policy or information signals that are active in the current month.

2. Predict current behavioural outcomes.
   Using the current fixed attributes, behavioural states, and exogenous context inputs, the simulator evaluates the choice module and obtains the current behavioural outcomes $y_i(t)$.

3. Update group-level incidence.
   The predicted behaviours are aggregated to the locality group level and used to update incidence through the incidence-feedback equation.

4. Run weekly social-interaction rounds.
   Within the same monthly tick, the simulator performs four social-interaction rounds. In each round it:
   - samples recurring and one-shot contacts;
   - applies peer pull on the eligible recurring-contact ties;
   - updates descriptive norms from observed behaviour in recurring and one-shot contacts.

5. Update the remaining behavioural states.
   After the weekly social rounds are completed, the simulator updates the remaining behavioural states through partial adjustment toward their current targets.

6. Finalize the next-period state.
   The updated behavioural states become the starting point for the next monthly tick.

## Runtime parameter envelope

The simulator operates over the following parameter families. Parameters are grouped by the ABM component in which they enter. The table reports the full runtime bounds, the baseline default value, and the main provenance category. More specific calibration anchors are summarized below.

| Component | Parameter | Runtime bounds | Default | Provenance | Role in calibration |
|---|---|---:|---:|---|---|
| Social-response heterogeneity | Norm threshold prior $a_\theta$ | $(0,20]$ | $2.0$ | design | Governs the dispersion of agent-specific norm thresholds drawn from the Beta prior. |
| Social-response heterogeneity | Norm threshold bounds $\theta_{\min},\theta_{\max}$ | each in $[0,1]$ | $(0.05,0.95)$ | design | Set the admissible range for descriptive-norm thresholds. |
| State dynamics | Fast relaxation $\rho_{\text{fast}}$ | $[0,1]$ | $0.081$ | design | Governs the speed of partial adjustment for faster-moving constructs. |
| State dynamics | Slow relaxation $\rho_{\text{slow}}$ | $[0,1]$, with $\rho_{\text{slow}}\le \rho_{\text{fast}}$ | $0.025$ | design | Governs the speed of partial adjustment for slower-moving constructs. |
| Social interaction | Social convergence scaling $k_{\text{social}}$ | $[0,2]$ | $0.3$ | survey-anchored | Governs the strength of descriptive-norm updating by scaling the baseline social susceptibility $\lambda_i$. |
| Social interaction | Norm response slope $\kappa$ | $(0,25]$ | $8.0$ | design | Controls how sharply descriptive-norm updating turns on around the threshold $\theta_i$. |
| Social interaction | State-update noise $\sigma_{\text{social}}$ | $[0,0.04]$ | $0.006$ | design | Gaussian noise scale used in social and descriptive-norm updates. |
| Social interaction | Homophily decay $\gamma_h$ | $[0,10]$ | $1.5$ | calibrated | Controls how sharply peer-pull interactions are concentrated among similar agents. |
| Social interaction | Cross-locality bridging probability $b$ | $[0,1]$ | $0.08$ | calibrated | Controls how often static network ties are assigned outside the agent’s own locality. |
| Incidence feedback | Incidence memory $\gamma$ | $[0,1]$ | $0.58$ | calibrated | Controls how quickly incidence decays in the absence of continued growth. |
| Incidence feedback | Incidence growth rate $\beta$ | $[0,4]$ | $1.26$ | calibrated | Sets the baseline growth rate of the bounded incidence process before behavioural mitigation. |
| Incidence feedback | Vaccine mitigation weight $\alpha_v$ | $[0,1]$ | $0.42$ | external empirical | Scales how group-level vaccination uptake reduces local incidence growth. |
| Incidence feedback | NPI mitigation weight $\alpha_p$ | $[0,1]$ | $0.52$ | external empirical | Scales how group-level NPI uptake reduces local incidence growth. |
| Incidence feedback | Incidence baseline $I_0$ | $[0,1]$ | $0.05$ | design | Baseline incidence anchor used in the feedback from local incidence to perceived stakes. |
| Incidence feedback | Incidence-to-stakes elasticity $k_{\text{fb}}$ | $[0,2]$ | $1.35$ | calibrated | Controls how strongly local incidence deviations are translated into behavioural salience. |

In this table, survey-anchored denotes parameters tied to survey-derived quantities or survey-motivated behavioural timescales; external empirical denotes parameters anchored to evidence outside the survey, such as vaccine- and NPI-effectiveness estimates; calibrated denotes parameters chosen to reproduce plausible aggregate wave growth, decay, or network-mixing behaviour under the survey-grounded dynamics; and design denotes parameters set as modelling choices to define admissible ranges or prior distributions.

$k_{\text{social}}$ is anchored to a monthly social-convergence timescale informed by small-effect social-norm evidence. $\beta$ and $\gamma$ are calibrated to reproduce plausible monthly epidemic-wave growth and decay patterns, using OWID-style case trajectories as an external envelope. $k_{\text{fb}}$ is calibrated as an effective incidence-to-stakes elasticity, informed by survey evidence linking epidemic conditions to danger and fear appraisals. $\gamma_h$ and $b$ are calibrated from generic homophily and small-world network reasoning. The mitigation weights $\alpha_v$ and $\alpha_p$ are anchored to external empirical evidence on the incidence-reducing effects of vaccination and bundled non-pharmaceutical interventions, respectively.

Scenario forcing is specified separately through the exogenous term $F_g(t)$ rather than through a single scalar parameter. In the baseline configuration this includes an importation floor and, where activated by the scenario, optional local bumps or periodic pulses with user-defined amplitude, period, width, and start time. Because these quantities define intervention schedules rather than stable structural parameters, they are reported with the scenarios rather than in the main parameter table.



# Sensitivity screening by Morris elementary effects {#sec-appendix-sensitivity-screening}

The aim of sensitivity screening is to assess whether the intervention comparisons in @sec-05-agent-based-model depend strongly on particular uncertain parameters, and to identify which broad mechanism families matter most for those comparisons.

For each scenario $s$ and parameter vector $\theta$, let $y_s(\theta)$ denote a scalar summary of the simulated outcome, computed either at the final tick or as an area-under-the-curve summary. I study intervention effects relative to their reference baseline,
$$
\Delta_s(\theta)=y_s(\theta)-y_{\mathrm{ref}(s)}(\theta).
$$

Most scenarios are compared with the standard baseline, while scenarios defined under importation are compared with the corresponding importation baseline. Within each parameter setting, baseline and intervention runs share the same seeds and contact network, so the estimated deltas are less affected by Monte Carlo noise.

The screening varies the main uncertain behavioural and epidemiological parameters jointly over plausible ranges and applies the Morris method of elementary effects [@morris_1991_factorial_sampling; @campolongo_2007_effective]. For parameter $j$, the elementary effect is
$$
EE_j=
\frac{\Delta_s(\theta^{(t+1)})-\Delta_s(\theta^{(t)})}
{\theta_j^{(t+1)}-\theta_j^{(t)}}.
$$

I summarise these effects with the standard Morris statistics: $\mu^*$, the mean absolute elementary effect, and $\sigma$, the dispersion of the elementary effects across sampled trajectories. Large $\mu^*$ indicates that a parameter is an important source of uncertainty for the scenario effect, while large $\sigma$ indicates that its influence varies more across the parameter space, consistent with nonlinearity, interactions, or context dependence. To keep the results substantively interpretable, the reported heatmap aggregates parameter-level $\mu^*$ values to four broad mechanism families: persistence, social diffusion, incidence feedback, and stochasticity.



# Code and reproducibility {#sec-appendix-code}

The codebase includes data preparation, construction of derived variables and indices, estimation of the empirical models, the LLM micro-validation framework, and the agent-based model used for the counterfactual experiments. The thesis code is available in the public GitHub repository ([beam-preventive-behavior-abm](https://github.com/adamamer20/beam-preventive-behavior-abm)), while the full survey data are not publicly available.
